title: Recognize fractions greater than 1
description: Identify the fraction of a whole that is shaded.  Fractions are greater than or equal to one.

assessment_items.question_content:
Each circle represents $1$ whole.



[[☃ image 1]]

**What fraction does the total area shaded represent?**  

[[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
The $\purpleD{\text{numerator}}$ is the number of equal parts being counted.

The $\blueD{\text{denominator}}$ is the number of equal parts in **one** whole. 

$\dfrac{\purpleD{\text{numerator}}}{\blueD{\text{denominator}}}$

Each whole is divided into $\blueD6$ equal pieces. So the $\blueD{\text{denominator}}$ is $\blueD6$.

$\purpleD{\dfrac{?}\blueD{6}}$

There are $\purpleD{10}$ pieces shaded. So, the $\purpleD{\text{numerator}}$ is $\purpleD{10}$.

$\purpleD{\dfrac{10}\blueD{6}}$
The shaded amount is equal to $\purpleD{\dfrac{10}\blueD{6}}$.

options.alt: Two circles. Each circle is divided into 6 equal parts. The first circle has 6 parts shaded. The second circle has 4 parts shaded.

assessment_items.question_content:

There are $\purpleD{5}$ pieces shaded. So, the $\purpleD{\text{numerator}}$ is $\purpleD{5}$.

$\purpleD{\dfrac{5}\blueD{4}}$
The shaded amount is equal to $\purpleD{\dfrac{5}\blueD{4}}$.

**Which images show $\dfrac3{2}$ shaded?**

The $\blueD{\text{denominator}}$ is $\blueD2$, so we need $\blueD2$ equal pieces in each whole.

The $\purpleD{\text{numerator}}$ is $\purpleD{3}$, so we need $\purpleD3$ equal parts shaded.
